summary: Gruber reviews Apple's September 2021 'California Streaming' event, covering the iPhones 13, Apple Watch Series 7, and new iPads. He finds the California scenic theme pretty but unmotivated, details the camera spec differences between Pro and non-Pro iPhone models, and notes the Apple Watch Series 7 as a minor refresh. He highlights the iPad Mini's impressive leap to modern design and A15 chip. The piece closes with a pointed observation that Apple should grow Services through original products like TV+ and Fitness+ rather than depending on App Store rent-seeking.
key_points: The iPhone 13 Pro and non-Pro models now have meaningfully different camera systems, unlike last year's shared-case similarity, with the Pro getting better apertures and macro photography
Apple Watch Series 7 is a minor refresh, and Apple played unfair with its text comparison screenshots by using tighter line spacing on the Series 7
The new iPad Mini is essentially an iPad Air Mini, adopting modern design, USB-C, A15 chip with 5-core GPU, and Touch ID on the power button
Apple's reliance on App Store rent-seeking for Services revenue growth is culturally unhealthy compared to building original products people want to pay for
money_quotes: Standing on stage, alone, at the San Diego Symphony's outdoor theater, in front of all those empty seats. It was both beautiful and an instant reminder of what we're all missing.
The AI-driven automatic focus changes in Cinematic Mode video seem too good to be true. Very futuristic feature, if it works as promised.
Apple doesn't usually play games like that in comparisons. Yellow card issued.
The iPad Mini is really more like an iPad Air Mini.
Getting 30 percent of the revenue from subscriptions to other company's services is a fine business, financially, but it's like junk food for any company's culture.

Apple is a great company because they make great original things that people want to pay for. TV+ and Fitness+ are exactly that. Collecting 30 percent of another company's in-app subscription revenue is not.
key_insight: Apple's cultural strength comes from making original products people want to pay for, not from collecting rent on other companies' subscriptions.
